title: Agents, MCP & LangGraph
description: Agentic patterns, MCP, LangGraph, tools, guardrails, safety.

cards.front: What is an LLM agent, and how is it different from a chatbot?
cards.back: A chatbot maps prompt → text. An agent uses an LLM as a reasoning engine that takes actions through tools, observes results, and loops until a goal is met (the ReAct loop: Reason → Act → Observe → repeat). Components: an LLM for planning, tools, memory (short and long term), and a control loop. The leap is acting and iterating, not just answering.
distractors: An agent is a chatbot fine-tuned on more data; both only map a prompt to a text reply.
An agent is a chatbot that runs on a server instead of a phone, with no other difference.
An agent generates the whole plan up front in one response and never observes results.
An agent is a smaller, faster model used to route requests to the real chatbot.
An agent replaces the LLM with hard-coded rules; the LLM only formats the final text.
An agent is a chatbot with a bigger context window, which is what lets it use tools.
cards.quizAnswer: An LLM that takes actions via tools, observes results, and loops toward a goal — not just mapping prompt to text.

cards.front: What is MCP and what problem does it solve?
cards.back: Model Context Protocol — an open standard connecting LLMs to external tools and data through a uniform interface ('USB-C for AI tools'). A server exposes tools (callable functions), resources (readable data), and prompts (templates); any MCP client/host can discover and use them. It solves the N×M integration explosion: build each tool once, use it with any client.
distractors: A model-compression protocol that shrinks context windows to fit more tools in memory.
A proprietary API from one vendor for calling that vendor's models only.
A prompt format that embeds tool instructions directly in the system prompt as plain text.
A multi-cloud provider that hosts models across AWS, Azure, and GCP simultaneously.
A caching layer that stores tool outputs so models avoid recomputing them.
A training method that bakes tool definitions into the model's weights to avoid wiring.
cards.quizAnswer: An open standard (USB-C for AI tools) where servers expose tools/resources so any client reuses them — solving N×M integration.

cards.front: How does an LLM 'know' how to call a tool? (Function/tool calling)
cards.back: The model is trained for structured tool calling: you provide tool definitions as a schema (name, description, JSON-schema parameters). When it decides to use one, it emits a structured tool-call object (name + arguments) instead of prose; your orchestration executes it and feeds the result back as a tool message. Good descriptions/schemas and argument validation make it reliable.
distractors: You parse the model's free-text reply with regular expressions to guess which tool it meant.
The model executes the function itself inside its weights and returns only the result.
Tools are hard-coded in a fixed order, and the model just signals 'next' to advance.
The model is shown example outputs and copies the closest one verbatim as the call.
Tool calls are chosen by a separate classifier model, never by the main LLM.
The host picks the tool randomly and the model only fills in the arguments afterward.
cards.quizAnswer: You give tool schemas; the model emits a structured tool-call object (name + arguments) that your code executes and feeds back.

cards.front: Why LangGraph over a simple loop of prompts?
cards.back: Real agents need state, control flow, and recovery. LangGraph models the agent as a stateful graph: nodes are steps, edges define transitions (including conditional/cyclic), and a shared state object is threaded through. Benefits: explicit control flow and cycles, durable inspectable state, human-in-the-loop pause/resume, checkpointing, observability, and natural guardrail placement.
distractors: LangGraph is a faster LLM that replaces the need for prompts entirely.
It is a vector database optimized for storing agent conversation history.
It compiles prompts into a single mega-prompt so the agent makes only one LLM call.
It removes loops and branches, forcing every agent to run as a strict linear chain.
It is a hosting service that runs your agent on managed GPUs for lower latency.
It auto-generates the agent's tools from the database schema at runtime.
cards.quizAnswer: It models the agent as a stateful graph with cycles, persistence, human-in-the-loop, and observability — beyond a naive loop.

cards.front: How do you stop a runaway agent? (Guardrails & safety)
cards.back: Defense in depth: iteration caps / step budgets, timeouts and cost ceilings, tool allow-listing with least privilege (e.g. read-only DB creds), input/output validation, sandboxing code execution, human-in-the-loop approval for irreversible actions, confirmation/dry-runs for destructive ops, and full observability/logging for audit.
distractors: Lower the temperature to 0 so the agent becomes deterministic and cannot loop.
Give the agent broad admin permissions so it can fix its own mistakes mid-run.
Trust a well-written system prompt to fully prevent any unsafe or runaway behavior.
Run the agent on a faster GPU so any infinite loop completes before causing harm.
Disable logging to reduce overhead, since traces slow the control loop down.
Let the agent self-approve high-impact actions to avoid blocking on a human.
cards.quizAnswer: Defense in depth: iteration/cost caps, least-privilege tools, input/output validation, sandboxing, and human approval for risky actions.

cards.front: What's the security model / threat surface of tool-using agents?
cards.back: Treat the LLM as an untrusted, manipulable component; security lives in the deterministic layer around it. Key risks: prompt injection (malicious instructions in retrieved content/tool output — the #1 risk), SSRF/unsafe tool inputs, excessive agency/over-permissioned tools, sensitive data exposure, and insecure output handling. Mitigate with least privilege, input/output validation, sandboxing, and human gates.
distractors: The main risk is the model's weights being stolen, so encryption of the checkpoint solves it.
Agents are safe as long as the LLM provider is reputable; the app layer adds no risk.
Prompt injection is fully solved by instructing the model to 'ignore malicious instructions.'
Give the agent the same permissions as the most privileged user so it never gets blocked.
Security is best handled inside the prompt; deterministic checks are redundant overhead.
Tool outputs are inherently trusted because they come from your own systems.
cards.quizAnswer: Treat the LLM as untrusted: defend against prompt injection, SSRF, excessive agency, and data leaks in the deterministic layer.

cards.back: Single agent: one LLM with a set of tools — simpler, cheaper, easier to debug, and the right default. Multi-agent: specialized agents collaborate (e.g. a supervisor delegating to workers) — separation of concerns and parallelism, but more complex, expensive, and harder to debug, with errors/latency compounding. Don't reach for multi-agent until a single well-equipped agent demonstrably can't cope.
distractors: Multi-agent systems are always cheaper because each agent uses a smaller model.
Single-agent systems can't use tools; that's the whole reason multi-agent exists.
Multi-agent is the right default; a single agent is only for trivial demos.
Multi-agent systems are easier to debug because each agent is isolated.
The two are identical at runtime; 'multi-agent' is just a marketing term.
Multi-agent eliminates latency by running every agent on a separate GPU in parallel.
cards.quizAnswer: Single agent is the simpler, cheaper default; reach for multi-agent (e.g. a supervisor) only when one demonstrably can't cope.

cards.back: Short-term/working memory is the conversation history and scratchpad in the context window (bounded, so you truncate or summarize older turns). Long-term memory persists across sessions, usually in a vector store (embed facts/past interactions, retrieve relevant ones — RAG over the agent's own history) or a structured store. It's fundamentally a context-management problem: keep, summarize, or offload.
distractors: Agent memory is the GPU's VRAM; more VRAM directly means the agent remembers more turns.
Long-term memory means fine-tuning the model on each conversation after it ends.
Memory is unlimited because modern context windows hold the entire history verbatim.
Short-term memory is stored in a vector DB and long-term memory in the prompt.
Memory works by caching the KV-cache to disk and reloading it next session.
Agents have no memory; each tool call starts a completely fresh model instance.
cards.quizAnswer: Short-term working memory in the context window (summarized) plus long-term memory in a vector store — a context-management problem.

cards.front: What is structured output and why does it matter for agents?
cards.back: Forcing the model to return data conforming to a schema (e.g. JSON matching a Pydantic model) instead of free prose, so downstream code can parse it deterministically. Enforced via constrained/grammar-based decoding (only tokens that keep JSON valid), provider JSON mode / function-calling schemas, or validate-and-retry. It's the seam between the probabilistic AI layer and deterministic systems.
distractors: Formatting the model's prose answer with markdown headings for readability.
Limiting the output to a fixed number of tokens so responses stay short.
Making the model output the same text every time by setting temperature to 0.
Returning the raw token IDs so the application can decode them itself.
Structured output is purely cosmetic and has no effect on agent reliability.
Storing the output in a structured database table after the model replies.
cards.quizAnswer: Forcing schema-valid output (e.g. JSON/Pydantic) so downstream code can parse it; enforced by constrained decoding or validation.
